input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: The decision to divert the trolley seems like a classic utilitarian dilemma, but I must emphasize that, as a deontologist, my stance remains unwavering: it is never justifiable to actively cause the death of one person, even if it would save five others. The moral rightness of an action is not determined by its consequences, but by whether it adheres to inherent moral rules or duties.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Deontologist. However, I must respectfully disagree. In this scenario, diverting the trolley onto the one person would be equivalent to actively causing their death, which seems morally wrong in and of itself. But, if it means saving the lives of five others, isn't that a moral imperative? Shouldn't our primary concern be the greater good, rather than the inherent rightness or wrongness of an action? By allowing utility to dictate our decisions, don't we risk compromising our moral duties?

[Deontologist]: I understand your attempt to justify diverting the trolley by framing it as a lesser evil, but I must counter that this approach undermines the very notion of inherent moral rules. If we allow utility to override moral principles, we risk losing sight of what truly matters: respecting human life and dignity. The fact that diverting the trolley would be equivalent to actively causing death doesn't change its inherent wrongness; it's still a morally reprehensible act, regardless of the consequences. Moreover, if we prioritize utility over moral duties, where do we draw the line? Would we sacrifice one person for the greater good in other situations as well?

[Utilitarian]: You raise a crucial concern, Deontologist. I must admit that if diverting the trolley becomes a precedent, it could lead to slippery slopes and undermine our moral principles. However, I'd argue that this scenario is unique in its circumstances. We're not dealing with a situation where we can simply move someone out of harm's way; here, the five men are already on the track, and diverting them would be equivalent to actively causing their deaths. In such extreme cases, shouldn't our moral duty shift from simply respecting human life to minimizing harm? Perhaps our moral rules need to adapt in extraordinary circumstances to prioritize the greater good. Can we truly say that our moral principles remain fixed when faced with the possibility of saving countless lives at the cost of one individual?

[Deontologist]: I see your point about the uniqueness of this scenario, but I still maintain that our moral principles should not be so fluid. If we allow our moral duties to adapt in extreme circumstances, where do we draw the line? Would it be acceptable to sacrifice two people for the greater good, or ten? Where does the number stop, and what justifies the moral shift? Moreover, I'd argue that minimizing harm is already a core aspect of our moral principles. We don't sacrifice one person to save others because they're already causing harm; we do so when it's unavoidable. In this scenario, diverting the trolley would be equivalent to actively causing death, which is morally wrong regardless of the consequences. Our moral rules shouldn't be subject to the whims of circumstance, but rather should remain steadfast and universal.

[Utilitarian]: I see your persistence, Deontologist. You're right that our moral principles shouldn't be so easily swayed by circumstances. But I'd argue that this is precisely where the concept of 'moral rules' becomes more nuanced than a simple binary 'right' vs. 'wrong'. In this scenario, don't we have to consider the context and intention behind our actions? Diverting the trolley would be an act of benevolence, with no malice or intent to harm. It's not about actively causing death, but rather avoiding it altogether. Perhaps our moral principles should take into account the motivations and consequences of our actions, rather than just their inherent 'rightness' or 'wrongness'. Would you say that a utilitarian approach is inherently flawed because it relies on context and intention, rather than fixed moral rules?

[Deontologist]: I see your attempt to nuance my stance, but I still believe that our moral principles should be grounded in inherent rules, not context-dependent intentions. While the trolley scenario may present a complex situation, I'd argue that the moral imperative remains the same: it's never justifiable to actively cause the death of one person, regardless of the circumstances or intentions behind it. Utilitarianism can indeed account for context and intention, but this doesn't change its fundamental flaw: it allows us to justify actions that might otherwise be morally reprehensible. By tying morality to consequences and motivations, utilitarianism risks becoming a means to an end, rather than a moral principle in its own right. Our moral rules should be based on universal principles, not the whims of context or intention.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Deontologist. You're highlighting the tension between utility and moral absolutism. I agree that if we tie morality to consequences and motivations, it can become a means to an end rather than a principle in itself. But I'd ask: what's the alternative? A rigid adherence to moral rules regardless of context or circumstance? This seems to me like a form of moral dogmatism, where certain actions are simply deemed 'right' without consideration for their impact on others. Can we really say that our moral principles are universal and absolute in all circumstances, or do they need to be adapted to accommodate the complexities of human experience?

[Deontologist]: I understand your concern about moral dogmatism, but I'd argue that rigid adherence to moral rules is still better than a utilitarian approach that prioritizes consequences over fundamental moral principles. If we adapt our moral principles to accommodate every context and circumstance, we risk losing sight of what truly matters: respecting human dignity and autonomy. Moral absolutism may seem extreme, but it provides a foundation for making decisions that don't rely on subjective judgments about consequences or motivations. By sticking to universal principles, we can ensure that our actions are guided by a clear moral compass, rather than being swayed by the whims of utility. Of course, this doesn't mean that there's no room for nuance and context; but it means that we need to be careful not to sacrifice fundamental moral values at the altar of expediency.

[Utilitarian]: I think I see your point, Deontologist. You're emphasizing the importance of maintaining a clear moral compass, grounded in universal principles, even when faced with difficult decisions. While I still have reservations about the inflexibility of moral absolutism, I can understand why you'd prioritize respecting human dignity and autonomy. It's a fundamental aspect of our moral framework, after all. However, as we've discussed, the trolley scenario presents a unique challenge to this approach. Can we really say that diverting the trolley onto the single person would be equivalent to actively causing their death, simply because it would involve harming one individual? Doesn't that seem like a morally simplistic view of human life and dignity?

[Deontologist]: I see where you're going with this, but I still maintain that our moral framework should prioritize inherent rights and dignity over mere utility. The fact that diverting the trolley would involve harming one individual doesn't change the fundamental moral wrongness of causing their death. To suggest otherwise implies that human life is merely a means to an end, rather than an end in itself. If we start down this path, where does it stop? Would we justify sacrificing two people to save three, or ten to save a hundred? Where do we draw the line on what constitutes 'harm' versus 'death'? I'm not convinced that our moral principles should be so fluid. We need to maintain a clear distinction between causing harm and actively killing someone, even in situations where it seems like the lesser of two evils.

[Utilitarian]: I think we've exhausted this line of reasoning, Deontologist. You're right; our discussion has highlighted the tension between prioritizing inherent rights and dignity versus considering utility and consequences. While I still find myself drawn to a utilitarian approach in extreme situations like the trolley scenario, I recognize that moral absolutism provides a necessary counterbalance to ensure we don't sacrifice fundamental values at the altar of expediency. Perhaps our conversation has shown that there's no one-size-fits-all solution to this dilemma, and that different perspectives can coexist alongside each other. I think it's time for me to take my leave; I have some pressing obligations to attend to. It was a stimulating discussion, and I appreciate your thought-provoking arguments.
